message:
test(core): property-based tests for session topology invariants (wa-j0fw)

18 proptest properties covering PaneNode pane_count/collect_pane_ids/serde
with recursive strategy (depth 3, 32 nodes), TopologySnapshot serde/empty/
pane_count/to_json/from_json, WindowSnapshot and TabSnapshot serde,
InferenceQuality distinctness, split ratio sums, and field preservation.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -873,7 +873,7 @@
 {"id":"wa-ixt4","title":"Create WezTerm fork with wa feature flag and minimal integration points","description":"## Overview

Fork WezTerm and add the minimal code needed to support wa event delivery. All changes are behind \`#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]\`.

## Prerequisites

- Completed: wa-wyr1 (WaEventSink trait design)

## Repository Setup

### 1. Fork WezTerm
\`\`\`bash
gh repo fork wez/wezterm --clone
cd wezterm
git checkout -b feature/wa-integration
\`\`\`

### 2. Add Feature Flag
In Cargo.toml (workspace root):
\`\`\`toml
[workspace.features]
wa-integration = []
\`\`\`

In relevant crates (mux/Cargo.toml, wezterm/Cargo.toml):
\`\`\`toml
[features]
wa-integration = []
\`\`\`

## Modification Points

### 1. mux/src/lib.rs — Event Sink Registration

\`\`\`rust
#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
mod wa_events;

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
use wa_events::WaEventSink;

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
static WA_EVENT_SINK: OnceCell<Arc<dyn WaEventSink>> = OnceCell::new();

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
pub fn register_wa_event_sink(sink: Arc<dyn WaEventSink>) {
    WA_EVENT_SINK.set(sink).ok();
}

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
pub(crate) fn emit_wa_event(event: impl FnOnce(&dyn WaEventSink)) {
    if let Some(sink) = WA_EVENT_SINK.get() {
        event(sink.as_ref());
    }
}
\`\`\`

### 2. mux/src/pane.rs — Emit Output Events

In the method that receives PTY output:
\`\`\`rust
fn process_pty_output(&mut self, data: &[u8]) {
    // Existing WezTerm code...
    
    #[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
    crate::emit_wa_event(|sink| {
        sink.on_pane_output(self.pane_id, data);
    });
}
\`\`\`

### 3. mux/src/pane.rs — Emit State Changes

In methods that update pane state:
\`\`\`rust
fn set_title(&mut self, title: String) {
    // Existing code...
    
    #[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
    self.emit_state_change();
}

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
fn emit_state_change(&self) {
    crate::emit_wa_event(|sink| {
        sink.on_pane_state_change(self.pane_id, &self.get_wa_state());
    });
}
\`\`\`

### 4. mux/src/pane.rs — Emit User-Var Changes

In the OSC 1337 handler:
\`\`\`rust
fn set_user_var(&mut self, name: String, value: String) {
    // Existing code...
    
    #[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
    crate::emit_wa_event(|sink| {
        sink.on_user_var_changed(self.pane_id, &name, &value);
    });
}
\`\`\`

### 5. wezterm/src/main.rs — Initialize Sink

\`\`\`rust
#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
fn init_wa_integration() {
    if let Ok(socket_path) = std::env::var("WEZTERM_WA_SOCKET") {
        match wa_socket_sink::connect(&socket_path) {
            Ok(sink) => mux::register_wa_event_sink(sink),
            Err(e) => log::warn!("Failed to connect to wa socket: {}", e),
        }
    }
}
\`\`\`

## Testing the Fork

### 1. Build with Feature
\`\`\`bash
cargo build --features wa-integration
\`\`\`

### 2. Verify Default Build Unaffected
\`\`\`bash
cargo build  # Should work identically to upstream
\`\`\`

### 3. Integration Test
\`\`\`bash
# Start wa watch (which creates the socket)
wa watch &

# Start WezTerm with wa integration
WEZTERM_WA_SOCKET=/tmp/wa/events.sock ./target/debug/wezterm

# Verify events received
\`\`\`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] WezTerm fork created with feature/wa-integration branch
- [ ] Feature flag added to workspace
- [ ] Event emission added to pane output path
- [ ] Event emission added to state change paths
- [ ] Event emission added to user-var handler
- [ ] Default build (no feature) compiles and runs unchanged
- [ ] Feature build compiles and runs
- [ ] Events actually received by wa (integration test)

## Risks

- WezTerm internal APIs may change — minimize touchpoints
- Performance regression — benchmark before/after
- Thread safety bugs — careful with event emission

## Files to Modify (in WezTerm fork)

- Cargo.toml (workspace) — add feature
- mux/Cargo.toml — add feature
- mux/src/lib.rs — event sink registration
- mux/src/pane.rs — event emission
- wezterm/Cargo.toml — add feature
- wezterm/src/main.rs — initialization
- NEW: mux/src/wa_events.rs — trait + socket sink implementation

## References

- WezTerm mux: https://github.com/wez/wezterm/tree/main/mux
- Rust feature flags: https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/features.html","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CopperLantern","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:49:14.103113431Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.308598-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-10T06:52:05.653011Z","close_reason":"Superseded by wa-2umk2: FrankenTerm in-tree integration replaces minimal fork strategy","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-ixt4","depends_on_id":"wa-bjm9","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:21Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-ixt4","depends_on_id":"wa-wyr1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:21Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-iy98","title":"CLI: wa secrets scan/report","description":"## What
Add CLI commands to run scans and view reports.

## Why

## How

- `wa secrets report` prints counts and redacted locations

## Success Criteria
- Output is fully redacted and includes counts by pattern","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:16:30.346782578Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.307187-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-09T16:07:50.604246197Z","close_reason":"done","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-iy98","depends_on_id":"wa-59cb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:21Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-iy98","depends_on_id":"wa-vqql","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:21Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-iy98","depends_on_id":"wa-5yl1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:21Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-iza2","title":"Idempotent wezterm.lua patch: user-var forwarding lane to wa","description":"# Task: Idempotent `wezterm.lua` patch (user-var forwarding lane)

## Goal
Automate installation of a small `wezterm.lua` snippet that forwards wa-related `user-var-changed` events to wa.

## Why
- OSC 133 markers are good, but user-vars give us an extra deterministic channel.
- Forwarding is necessary for future features (explicit checkpoints, prompt boundaries, "agent ready" signals).
- Humans should not need to copy/paste Lua.

## Minimal forwarding lane (PLAN Appendix E.1)
This is the exact minimal Lua we must be able to install idempotently:

```lua
-- Forward user-var events to wa daemon
wezterm.on('user-var-changed', function(window, pane, name, value)
  if name:match('^wa%-') then
    wezterm.background_child_process {
      'wa', 'event', '--from-uservar',
      '--pane', tostring(pane:pane_id()),
      '--name', name,
      '--value', value
    }
  end
end)
```

Notes:
- Prefix filter: default is `wa-` (hyphen). If we later support `wa_`, the filter must remain strict and configurable.
- The background child process must be non-blocking for WezTerm UI responsiveness.

## Approach
- `wa setup` locates the active `wezterm.lua`.
- It applies an idempotent patch:
  - insert snippet if missing
  - do nothing if already present
- It creates a backup copy (non-destructive).

## Forwarding lane contract
- We only forward vars matching the configured prefix.
- The Lua code calls:
  - `wa event --from-uservar --pane <id> --name <name> --value <value>`
- wa decodes base64 payloads where applicable and emits an internal signal/event.

## Deliverables
- Lua snippet template (managed block with WA-BEGIN/WA-END markers).
- WezTerm config locator.
- Idempotent patcher.

## Testing
- Unit/fixture tests (see `wa-nu4.3.3.5`):
  - patcher inserts exactly one managed block
  - re-running is a no-op
  - backups are created and paths are reported
- E2E:
  - `wa-nu4.3.3.10` validates end-to-end idempotency in a temp HOME
  - `wa-4vx.10.14` validates user-var events flow through the forwarding lane

## Acceptance Criteria
- Re-running `wa setup` does not duplicate the snippet.
- Manual review of the file shows only the intended additions.

 {"id":"wa-j1ke","title":"[EPIC] Notification channels (Slack/Discord/email)","description":"## Background
wa already supports desktop notifications and generic webhooks (with Slack/Discord templates), but channel management is fragmented and lacks email and a unified interface.

## Goals
- Unify all notification delivery behind a single interface
- Extend channels to include email and per-channel config
- Ensure redaction and rate-limiting are applied before any send

## Non-Goals
- Full incident management system
- Replacing existing webhook templates (reuse them)

## Considerations
- Keep backward compatibility with existing [notifications] config
- Avoid logging secrets; apply redaction before dispatch
- Provide a test command to validate connectivity

## Success Criteria
- Configurable channels deliver alerts reliably
- Test command validates connectivity and redaction
- Unit + e2e tests with mocked endpoints","notes":"2026-02-07 progress: (1) Fixed notification config validation bug in wa-core so NotificationConfig::validate now calls email validation in crates/wa-core/src/config.rs with regression test coverage. (2) Fresh-eyes pass found brittle status contract test assumptions; patched crates/wa/tests/cli_contract_tests.rs so populated-plain status test accepts both successful pane output and actionable WezTerm-unavailable errors in fixture environments where WA_WEZTERM_CLI is set to /nonexistent/wezterm.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","assignee":"BoldSpring","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:07:48.504233794Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.189993-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-09T16:16:35.803836573Z","close_reason":"All children completed"}
 {"id":"wa-j3pu","title":"Web server hardening: localhost-only default, explicit bind, no secret leakage","description":"# Task: Web server hardening (safe-by-default)

## Goal
Ensure the optional `wa web` server is safe-by-default and hard to misuse.

## Threat model
Even “read-only” endpoints can leak:
- terminal transcripts (may contain secrets)
- tokens embedded in URLs
- hostnames, paths, and other sensitive metadata

So the web server must inherit wa’s redaction and safety conventions.

## Requirements
- **Bind defaults**
  - default bind: `127.0.0.1`
  - require an explicit, scary flag to bind publicly (proposed): `--dangerous-bind-any`
  - when binding non-localhost, print a prominent warning

- **No secret leakage**
  - all responses must be redacted using the same redaction pipeline as robot/audit/export
  - never include raw segment bytes by default

- **Hard limits**
  - request size limits
  - sensible default pagination limits

- **Surface area**
  - only GET endpoints in v0
  - no file-serving
  - no CORS-by-default (unless explicitly enabled)

## Testing strategy
- `cargo test --features web` includes cases that assert:
  - localhost bind is the default
  - public bind requires `--dangerous-bind-any`
  - redaction is applied to responses for any field that can contain secrets

## Acceptance Criteria
- A naive `wa web` run is not remotely reachable by default.
- Redaction is applied consistently (web == robot == audit == export).
","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T09:29:49.631105886Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.185233-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-07T08:02:59.636880052Z"}
 {"id":"wa-j7zq","title":"E2E: plan preview + workflow execution logs (no sleeps, rich artifacts)","description":"# Task: E2E plan preview + workflow execution logs

## Goal
Prove end-to-end that ActionPlan preview and execution logging work in a realistic run.

## Scenario
- Trigger a known workflow.
- Capture the ActionPlan preview (dry-run / prepare).
- Execute and verify:
  - plan is persisted
  - step logs exist and are queryable
  - failures (if induced) reference specific step boundaries

## Requirements
- No fixed sleeps; use wait-for/quiescence.
- Artifacts include:
  - plan JSON
  - step logs JSON
  - concise human summary

## Acceptance Criteria
- E2E produces deterministic plan and step logs.
- Artifacts are sufficient to debug without re-running interactively.

Will harden E2E to assert persisted action plans + workflow step logs + step-boundary failure evidence with artifacts.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T20:37:28.171529083Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.230726-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-08T19:33:27.272221438Z"}

 {"id":"wa-m76j","title":"FTUI-03.2 Enforce one-writer output routing for UI and log surfaces","description":"## Background\nfrankentui requires a one-writer rule. wa must eliminate direct stdout/stderr writes from active UI runtime paths.\n\n## Deliverables\n- unified output sink routing UI redraw + logs\n- explicit policy for subprocess output capture/routing\n- violation detection strategy in tests/CI\n\n## Acceptance Criteria\n- no direct terminal writes bypass output gate in migrated paths\n- sustained output does not corrupt cursor/layout state.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:07:51.521424086Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.287279-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-09T01:44:58.628915122Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-m76j","depends_on_id":"wa-f5wn","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:31Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-m79p","title":"Implement OpenAI/Codex device auth flow (auth.openai.com/codex/device)","description":"# Task: OpenAI device auth flow (Playwright)

## Goal
Automate the OpenAI device-code flow used by Codex CLI via Playwright in a way that is:
- robust to small UI changes
- safe with respect to secrets (no password storage)
- debuggable when it fails (screenshots/HTML dumps, redacted logs)

Target URL:
- `https://auth.openai.com/codex/device`

## Preconditions / inputs
- A persistent Playwright profile exists for the target account (profile dir derived from workspace/config).
- The workflow step has already obtained a `user_code` (device code) from the Codex pane.

## Happy path (v0)
1. Navigate to the device page.
2. Ensure we are in a logged-in state for the intended account:
   - if already logged in: proceed
   - if email prompt appears: fill email, continue
   - if password/MFA required: **do not attempt to automate**; exit into the fallback path (`wa-nu4.1.4.3`)
3. Fill the `user_code` form field.
4. Submit.
5. Verify success via a stable marker:
   - preferred: page contains “Successfully logged in” (or equivalent stable selector)
   - also capture URL + final DOM snapshot for debugging

## Failure modes
- Password/MFA prompts:
  - return a structured error that the workflow can treat as “interactive bootstrap required”
  - point to the fallback task (`wa-nu4.1.4.3`)
- Rate limit / bot detection / unexpected page:
  - fail with actionable error and attach redacted artifacts

## Logging / artifacts
- Never log passwords, session cookies, access tokens, or raw HTML that may contain secrets.
- On failure, write artifacts under the workspace artifacts dir:
  - screenshot
  - redacted DOM snapshot (or a hash + minimal selector debug)
  - a short, human-readable failure report

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - parse + validate the `user_code` format before browser automation (fail fast)
- Integration tests (offline):
  - run Playwright against a local HTML fixture that simulates the device page, verifying selector strategy
- Manual smoke tests:
  - a `wa browser smoke-test --openai-device` (tracked elsewhere) confirms it can complete on a real profile

## Acceptance Criteria
- Given a valid persistent profile that is already authenticated, this flow completes and returns “success” for a real account.
- If password/MFA is required, the flow exits safely with a clear “interactive bootstrap required” error and produces redacted artifacts.
","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T09:05:56.26018434Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.175965-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-29T15:36:02.425063332Z"}
 {"id":"wa-mhcd","title":"Implement Playwright auth flow for Anthropic (Claude Code /login)","description":"# Task: Implement Playwright auth flow for Anthropic (Claude Code `/login`)

## Goal
Provide an Anthropic login helper that can bring an account into an authenticated state using Playwright + persistent profiles.

Proposed API shape (adjust only if the browser spec bead requires it):
- `async fn complete_anthropic_auth(account: &AccountRecord, ctx: &mut BrowserAutomationContext) -> AuthOutcome`

Where `AuthOutcome` is one of:
- `Success`
- `NeedsHuman { redacted_url, instructions }`
- `Failed { reason, diagnostics }`

## Expected workflow integration
`handle_usage_limits` and `handle_auth_required` for `AgentType::ClaudeCode` will:
1. send `/login` to the pane
2. parse any printed URL/code if present
3. call this browser step
4. wait for pane to become idle / ready

## Implementation outline
### Happy path (already authenticated)
- Create/load persistent profile for `(service=anthropic, account_key)`.
- Navigate to a stable landing page (likely `https://claude.ai/` or console).
- Detect an "already logged in" selector; if present, return `Success`.

### Login path (no session)
- Navigate to the correct login page (start with `https://claude.ai/login` unless `/login` prints a more specific URL).
- If prompted for email, fill using account metadata.
- Continue until either:
  - we reach the authenticated state, OR
  - we detect password/MFA/SSO interstitial

### Password/MFA/SSO handling
- **Do not** ask for, store, or type passwords.
- If a password/MFA step appears, return `NeedsHuman` with:
  - page URL to complete manually (redacted)
  - explicit next-step instructions
  - reminder that after completion, the profile will persist and future runs can be automated

## Safety requirements
- Redact any URLs containing tokens or codes in logs.
- Cap total runtime with a top-level timeout.
- Always close contexts/pages on both success and failure (no zombie browsers).

## Testing
- Integration tests (offline):
  - run Playwright against local HTML fixtures representing:
    - already-authenticated state
    - needs-human state (password/MFA)
  - validate structured outcomes and redaction

- Manual smoke tests:
  - validate on a real machine with a pre-authenticated profile

## Deliverables
- Working Anthropic auth flow using Playwright persistent profiles.
- Clear `NeedsHuman` messaging that can be surfaced by `wa robot workflow ...`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- On a machine with a pre-authenticated profile, the flow returns `Success` without manual steps.
- On a machine without a profile, the flow either authenticates fully OR returns `NeedsHuman` without leaking secrets.

 {"id":"wa-ml1b","title":"Agent skills/docs: how to use wa via robot+MCP (swarm playbooks)","description":"# Task: Agent skills/docs (swarm playbooks)

## Goal
Write self-contained guidance for agent swarms to use wa effectively.

## Topics
- When to use `wa robot` vs MCP.
- Canonical flows:
  - check state
  - read text tail
  - search history
  - handle unhandled events
  - run workflows
- Safety expectations:
  - policy gates
  - avoid alt-screen
  - verify after sending

## Deliverables
- `skills/` docs (or equivalent) that can be copied into agent system prompts.

## Validation / tests
Agent playbooks must not drift.

- Prefer to keep examples aligned with:
  - robot JSON schemas (field names)
  - E2E scripts (canonical flows)

- If feasible, add a small CI check that:
  - runs a couple “playbook” robot commands against fixtures
  - asserts outputs are schema-parseable

## Acceptance Criteria
- A new agent can operate wa without human coaching.


## Testing
- Drift prevention:
  - Add a CI check (or unit test) that validates embedded robot JSON snippets against schemas.
  - Ensure docs examples are generated/updated from fixtures where possible to avoid bit-rot.
","notes":"Completed swarm playbooks docs + drift guard. Added docs/swarm-playbook.md (robot vs MCP guidance, canonical control loop, event triage mutation flow, workflow/send verification, safety rules, prompt snippet). Linked from README.md MCP section. Added docs smoke test smoke_robot_playbook_commands_emit_json_envelopes in crates/wa/tests/docs_smoke.rs to validate core playbook robot commands emit parseable JSON envelopes with boolean ok field. Validation performed: cargo fmt -p wa --check (pass); runtime smoke using existing target/debug/wa with isolated WA_WORKSPACE + WA_WEZTERM_CLI=/nonexistent/wezterm confirmed parseable JSON envelopes for robot state/events/workflow list. Workspace currently has unrelated wa-core compile blockers (E0603 in crates/wa-core/src/undo.rs, E0609 in crates/wa-core/src/storage.rs) preventing full cargo test/check/clippy in this mixed-agent branch.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T09:42:01.859702463Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.217664-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-08T19:46:25.000942122Z"}
 {"id":"wa-mlai","title":"Unit tests: search linting + FTS maintenance","description":"## Coverage
- Linting of invalid query forms
- Autocomplete suggestion stability
- FTS verify/rebuild idempotence

- Log query inputs and lint outputs
- Log rebuild steps and timings

 {"id":"wa-nu4.4.4.4","title":"Unstick workflow: use ast-grep to scan repo and propose next actions (optional)","description":"# Task: Unstick workflow (ast-grep integration)

## Goal
Provide a workflow that helps an agent that is stuck by running **structure-aware**, **read-only** scans and returning actionable suggestions.

The value proposition is: “when the agent doesn’t know what to do next, give it a constrained, high-signal map of likely fixes.”

## What this workflow does
Run a small, curated set of ast-grep queries such as:
- TODO/FIXME hotspots
- `unwrap()` / `expect()` / `panic!()` usage
- suspicious error handling patterns
- targeted patterns for the repo’s dominant languages (Rust first)

## Safety constraints (strict)
- Read-only only: no codemods and no file writes in v0.1.
- Do not allow arbitrary shell commands.
- If user-provided queries are supported, validate them and enforce:
  - timeouts
  - file count / file size limits
  - language whitelist

## Output contract
- Produce a structured report:
  - finding type
  - file:line
  - short snippet (bounded length)
  - suggested next action
- Human mode prints a concise summary.
- Robot mode returns a stable JSON payload.

## Testing strategy
- Fixture-based tests that:
  - run the workflow against a small fixture repo
  - assert findings are returned deterministically
  - assert no files are modified

## Acceptance Criteria
- Workflow runs ast-grep read-only and returns a concise, actionable report.
- Output is deterministic and bounded.
- Running the workflow does not modify any repository files.

 {"id":"wa-nwg","title":"Implement `wa why` command: explain any recent policy decision","description":"# Task: Implement `wa why` command

## Goal
Create a dedicated CLI command that explains any recent policy decision with full context.

## Why This Matters
When wa denies a send or takes unexpected action, users need to understand why. The `wa why` command provides that answer instantly without diving into logs.

## Command Interface
```bash
# Explain the most recent denial
$ wa why denied

# Explain a specific decision by ID
$ wa why denied --decision-id abc123

# Explain denial for a specific pane
$ wa why denied --pane 3

# Explain a workflow decision
$ wa why workflow-skipped --workflow handle_compaction

# JSON output for automation
$ wa why denied --pane 3 --format json
```

## Output Format
```
Decision: DENY
Type: SendText
Target: Pane 3 (claude_code @ /home/user/project)
Timestamp: 2026-01-18T14:32:01Z

Reason: Pane is in AltScreen mode
Rule: safety.alt_screen_block (severity: hard_deny)

Evidence:
  - AltScreen flag: true (detected at 14:31:55)
  - Last normal mode: 14:28:12
  - Pane title: "vim AGENTS.md"

Rationale:
  Sending input while AltScreen is active can corrupt TUI applications
  or cause unintended side effects. This is a safety guard.

To proceed:
  1. Wait for pane to exit AltScreen (close vim/less/etc.)
  2. Check pane state: wa status --pane 3
  3. If you truly need to override, use the allow-once approval flow:
     - rerun the original action to get an allow-once code
     - approve it: wa approve <code>
     - retry the action
```

## Implementation

### 1. Decision Context Storage
Extend the audit trail to store full decision context (see `wa-7wk`):
- conditions/capabilities
- rules evaluated
- determining rule
- evidence collected

### 2. Explanation Templates
Map `(rule_id, decision)` → explanation template:
```rust
struct ExplanationTemplate {
    reason: &'static str,
    rationale: &'static str,
    to_proceed: Vec<&'static str>,
}

static EXPLANATIONS: phf::Map<&str, ExplanationTemplate> = phf_map! {
    "safety.alt_screen_block:deny" => ExplanationTemplate {
        reason: "Pane is in AltScreen mode",
        rationale: "Sending input while AltScreen is active can corrupt TUI applications...",
        to_proceed: vec![
            "Wait for pane to exit AltScreen (close vim/less/etc.)",
            "Check pane state: wa status --pane {pane_id}",
            "If needed, use allow-once approvals: wa approve <code>",
        ],
    },
    // ... more templates
};
```

### 3. Query Interface
```rust
fn explain_decision(
    storage: &StorageHandle,
    decision_id: Option<&str>,
    pane_id: Option<PaneId>,
    decision_type: DecisionType,
) -> Result<Explanation, Error>;
```

### 4. CLI Integration
```rust
#[derive(Parser)]
struct WhyArgs {
    #[clap(subcommand)]
    decision_type: DecisionType,

    #[clap(long)]
    decision_id: Option<String>,

    #[clap(long)]
    pane: Option<PaneId>,

    #[clap(long, default_value = "text")]
    format: OutputFormat,
}
```

## Testing
- Unit tests: Each rule has a valid explanation template
- Integration tests: `wa why` returns expected explanations for known scenarios
- Template tests: All templates have placeholders filled correctly
- UX tests: Explanations are understandable (user testing)

## Acceptance Criteria
- `wa why denied` explains the most recent denial
- `wa why denied --pane 3` explains denial for specific pane
- All policy rules have explanation templates
- Output includes evidence, rationale, and "To proceed" steps
- JSON output is available for automation

 {"id":"wa-nxz0","title":"E2E script: RequireApproval → wa approve allow-once → send succeeds (audited)","description":"# Task: E2E script — approval allow-once

## Goal
Validate the full RequireApproval UX loop:
1) an action requires approval
2) wa returns a structured allow-once payload
3) a human grants approval via `wa approve`
4) the action succeeds on retry
5) the whole flow is auditable and redacted

This is the critical "human-in-the-loop" bridge that keeps wa safe and ergonomic.

## Scenario
- Use a policy config that forces RequireApproval for SendText in a safe situation (so this test is deterministic).
- Spawn a dummy pane that echoes received input.

Steps
1. Attempt a send (`wa send` or `wa robot send`): expect RequireApproval.
2. Extract `allow_once_code` from the output.
3. Run `wa approve <code> --yes`.
4. Retry the same send: expect Allow and successful injection.
5. Verify audit trail contains:
   - initial require-approval decision
   - approval grant
   - successful retry
   - redacted summaries (no raw secret-like input)

## Assertions
- RequireApproval returns a stable allow-once payload.
- Approval is scoped (wrong pane/action/workspace does not match).
- Retry succeeds only for the matching fingerprint.

## Artifacts
- Effective config used.
- Robot/human command outputs.
- Audit export JSONL (or query output).

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Logging
- Follow the standard E2E harness logging contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).
- Emit per-step start/end markers with timestamps + durations.
- Include case name and pane ids in every log line.
- Redact secrets (device codes, tokens) in logs and artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Script is deterministic.
- On failure, artifacts clearly distinguish:
  - policy decision bug
  - approval storage/lookup bug
  - audit/redaction bug


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Add a negative assertion: use the allow-once code against a different pane/workspace and ensure it is rejected.
  - If approvals have TTL, assert that an expired token is rejected (or explicitly document if TTL is not implemented).

- Artifact validation:
  - Assert audit artifacts include three distinct records (require-approval, approve, allowed retry) and are fully redacted.

 {"id":"wa-nyqf","title":"Implement `wa timeline` command: ASCII timeline with correlations","description":"# `wa timeline` command (ASCII timeline)

## Purpose
Display a unified event timeline in the terminal with an ASCII visualization and optional correlation markers.

## Usage
```bash
wa timeline [--last <duration>] [--pane <id>] [--type <event-type>] [--format {auto|plain|json}] [-v|-vv]
```

Notes:
- `--format` follows the global human CLI output convention (`wa-nu4.3.2.1`).
- `-v/-vv` controls how much detail is shown (event fields, correlation reasoning, etc.).

## Output format (plain example)
```
Timeline: 2026-01-18 14:00 - 14:30 (4 panes, 12 events)

14:02:15 ─┬─ Pane 1 (codex): session.started
          │
14:05:32 ─┼─ Pane 3 (claude): session.started
          │
14:12:01 ─┼─ Pane 1 (codex): session.compaction ──→ handled (14:12:08)
          │
14:28:03 ─┼─ Pane 1 (codex): codex.usage_limit_reached
          ├──→ workflow: handle_usage_limits (in progress)
          │
14:28:45 ─┴─ Pane 7 (codex-backup): session.started [CORRELATED: failover]

Legend: ──→ workflow action  [CORRELATED] cross-pane relationship
```

## Features
- Vertical timeline with timestamps.
- Events grouped by timestamp proximity.
- Workflow actions shown with arrows.
- Correlations highlighted with labels.
- Color coding in `--format auto` when stderr/stdout is a TTY.
- Legend for symbols.

## Options
- `--last`: Time range (e.g., `30m`, `2h`, `1d`).
- `--pane`: Filter to specific pane(s).
- `--type`: Filter to specific event types.
- `--format json`: Machine-readable output (schema-stable).
- `--format plain`: No ANSI output (stable for piping/snapshots).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Timeline displays events chronologically.
- [ ] Correlations are visually indicated.
- [ ] Handled events show workflow info.
- [ ] Filtering works correctly.
- [ ] JSON output is complete and schema-valid.

## Testing
- Unit tests for correlation heuristics and aggregation.
- Integration tests for timeline queries and rendering.
- E2E scenario verifies correlations with artifacts (see `wa-ugg`).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T17:50:50.689751477Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.303218-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-08T11:52:38.408820526Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-nyqf","depends_on_id":"wa-2vr1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:31Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-nyqf","depends_on_id":"wa-mxzp","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:31Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-nyqf","depends_on_id":"wa-tmia","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:31Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-nz6","title":"SQLite tables for snapshot storage and scrollback dedup","description":"## Goal
Extend wa's existing SQLite database (schema v20+) to store mux snapshots persistently, with efficient querying and retention management.

## Background
wa already has a mature SQLite storage layer with WAL mode, schema versioning, 13+ tables including panes, output_segments, events, agent_sessions, etc. The snapshot storage should follow the same patterns: epoch-ms timestamps, proper indexing, and retention policies.

## Design
1. New table `mux_snapshots`:
   - id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY
   - snapshot_id TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE (UUID)
   - created_at INTEGER NOT NULL (epoch ms)
   - trigger TEXT NOT NULL ('periodic', 'manual', 'pre_restart', 'pre_upgrade')
   - mux_server_pid INTEGER
   - wezterm_version TEXT
   - wa_version TEXT
   - window_count INTEGER
   - tab_count INTEGER
   - pane_count INTEGER
   - snapshot_json TEXT NOT NULL (full MuxSnapshot as JSON)
   - snapshot_size_bytes INTEGER
   - checksum TEXT (SHA-256 of snapshot_json for integrity)

2. New table `snapshot_scrollbacks`:
   - id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY
   - snapshot_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES mux_snapshots(snapshot_id)
   - pane_id INTEGER NOT NULL
   - content_hash TEXT NOT NULL (for dedup across snapshots)
   - content TEXT NOT NULL (escaped terminal output)
   - line_count INTEGER
   - byte_count INTEGER
   - UNIQUE(snapshot_id, pane_id)

3. Content-addressed scrollback dedup:
   - If two consecutive snapshots have the same scrollback hash for a pane, only store it once
   - Reference by content_hash to avoid duplicating multi-MB scrollback buffers

4. Retention policy:
   - Keep last N snapshots (configurable, default 10)
   - Keep all snapshots from last 24 hours
   - Keep at least 1 snapshot per day for last 7 days
   - Configurable via wa.toml [snapshots] section

5. Schema migration:
   - Increment schema version to 21 (or next available)
   - Add migration in storage.rs following existing patterns

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tables created on schema upgrade
- Insert and query snapshots with proper indexing
- Scrollback dedup verified (same content only stored once)
- Retention cleanup works correctly
- Migration from v20 to v21 is clean

## Benchmark & Property Testing Requirements

### Criterion Benchmarks
Add `benches/snapshot_sqlite.rs` using criterion:
- **insert_latency**: Benchmark inserting a full MuxSnapshot (50 panes, 5000 lines each) into the mux_snapshots and snapshot_scrollbacks tables. Measure wall-clock time and I/O bytes. Target: <500ms for a typical 50-pane snapshot.
- **query_latest_snapshot**: Benchmark querying the most recent snapshot by created_at. Target: <5ms with proper indexing.
- **query_by_trigger**: Benchmark querying snapshots filtered by trigger type. Target: <10ms.
- **dedup_savings**: Benchmark insert time when content_hash matches an existing row (dedup path) vs fresh insert. Measure storage savings for 10 consecutive snapshots where 80% of panes have unchanged scrollback.
- **retention_cleanup**: Benchmark the retention cleanup query (DELETE old snapshots) with 100, 500, 1000 snapshots in the table.

### Proptest
Add `tests/proptest_snapshot_sqlite.rs`:
- **schema_migration_correctness**: For any arbitrary initial database state (proptest generates random existing rows in pre-migration tables), assert that migration to v21 preserves all existing data and creates new tables without errors. Run the migration, then verify all pre-existing rows are intact and new tables exist with correct schema.
- **retention_policy_invariants**: For any sequence of snapshot inserts with arbitrary timestamps and trigger types (proptest generates), assert that after cleanup: (a) at most N snapshots remain, (b) all snapshots from the last 24 hours are retained, (c) at least 1 snapshot per day for the last 7 days is retained.
- **dedup_correctness**: For any sequence of (snapshot_id, pane_id, content) triples where some share the same content, assert that only one copy of each unique content exists in storage, and all references resolve correctly.

## Cross-References
- **wa-283h4.2** (WAL snapshots): The WAL (Write-Ahead Log) snapshot system provides a complementary persistence mechanism. While wa-nz6 stores discrete point-in-time snapshots, WAL snapshots capture a continuous log of state changes. The two systems should share the same SQLite database and coordinate schema versions. WAL snapshots can reference bd-nz6's snapshot_id as a "checkpoint" -- the WAL only needs to store deltas since the last full snapshot, dramatically reducing WAL size.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:17:31.871479Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T22:14:47.962935-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-10T20:46:44.982738-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-nz6","depends_on_id":"wa-rsaf","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:34:45.327547Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}

 {"id":"wa-v0d3","title":"Docs: secure distributed mode (setup, defaults, rotation, troubleshooting)","description":"# Task: Docs for secure distributed mode

## Goal
Document secure distributed mode so operators can enable it confidently.

## Content outline
- What distributed mode is (agent vs aggregator) and when to use it.
- Safe defaults:
  - local-only bind by default
  - explicit flags required for remote binds
- Security configuration:
  - token auth
  - TLS/mTLS setup (if enabled)
  - allowlist/identity model
- Rotation procedures:
  - how to rotate token/certs
  - how to verify effective config (`wa doctor`)
- Troubleshooting:
  - common failures and stable error codes
  - where to find logs and E2E artifacts

## Acceptance Criteria
- A future operator can deploy secure distributed mode without consulting PLAN.md.
- Docs are explicit about the safety model and how to avoid accidental exposure.

Updated README.md with a Secure Distributed Mode section and corrected limitations text to avoid contradicting distributed support.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T22:16:32.236305101Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.173547-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-06T16:58:55.574151901Z"}

 {"id":"wa-v3k5","title":"CLI: config profile list/create/apply","description":"## What

 {"id":"wa-v40a","title":"E2E script: backup/restore cycle (export, corrupt, import, verify)","description":"# E2E script: backup/restore cycle (export, corrupt, import, verify)

## Goal
Prove end-to-end that backup/restore is safe, user-friendly, and reliable:
- export produces a self-contained, verifiable backup artifact
- import rejects corrupt backups with actionable errors
- replace/merge semantics behave correctly
- pre-import safety backups are created when mutating an existing workspace
- no secrets leak into logs/artifacts

This is the system-level validation for `wa-1wg`.

## Key constraints
- Must follow the standard E2E harness contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and run via the standard runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).
- **No destructive shell cleanup** in test scripts (no `rm -rf`).
  - Use fresh temporary workspaces/directories per scenario to reset state.
- Deterministic synchronization:
  - no fixed sleeps as the primary sync primitive
  - wait on explicit conditions (segments/events present, watcher stopped, etc.) with bounded timeouts

## Test setup (harness)
- Use a dedicated E2E workspace root for this case.
- Each scenario uses its own fresh workspace dir:
  - `workspace_a/`, `workspace_b/`, …
- Generate minimal deterministic data:
  - spawn a dummy pane that prints a unique token and/or emits a known event
  - run `wa watch` long enough to persist at least one segment
  - stop watcher cleanly (`wa stop --workspace …` or SIGINT with bounded wait)

## Scenarios

### 1) Clean export/import round-trip
**Intent:** exported backup round-trips core data.

Steps:
1. Populate `workspace_a` with known captured data (at least one segment + one searchable token).
2. Export:
   - `wa backup export --workspace workspace_a --output artifacts/backup.wa`
3. Record a minimal evidence snapshot from `workspace_a` (stable fields only):
   - segment count (or “token is searchable” evidence)
   - event count (if applicable)
4. Import into a fresh `workspace_b`:
   - `wa backup import --workspace workspace_b artifacts/backup.wa --replace --yes`
5. Verify `workspace_b` matches the recorded evidence (stable fields only).

Assertions:
- backup file exists and is verifiable (`--verify` or checksum metadata).
- restored workspace contains the expected token/search hit.

### 2) Corrupt backup rejection
**Intent:** corruption is detected and rejected.

Steps:
1. Create a valid backup.
2. Corrupt it by appending bytes:
   - `printf 'garbage' >> artifacts/backup_corrupt.wa`
3. Import must fail:
   - `wa backup import --workspace workspace_b artifacts/backup_corrupt.wa …` (expected non-zero)

Assertions:
- error mentions integrity verification (checksum/manifest) and provides remediation.
- no partial restore occurs (workspace remains empty or unchanged).

### 3) Pre-import safety backup is created
**Intent:** importing into an existing workspace never destroys data without preserving a recovery artifact.

Steps:
1. Populate `workspace_c` with some data.
2. Export an “external” backup from `workspace_a` (or another workspace).
3. Import into `workspace_c` with replace mode:
   - `wa backup import --workspace workspace_c artifacts/external.wa --replace --yes`

Assertions:
- A pre-import backup file is created in the configured backup destination for `workspace_c`.
- The pre-import backup is itself verifiable.

### 4) Metadata-only backup
**Intent:** metadata-only export exists and is meaningfully smaller.

Steps:
1. Export full backup and metadata-only backup.
2. Compare file sizes.

Assertions:
- metadata-only file is smaller by a meaningful factor (threshold chosen to avoid flakiness).
- metadata-only backup still contains manifest/version info.

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.

## Artifacts
- `backup.wa`, `backup_corrupt.wa`, `external.wa`
- `doctor_workspace_paths.txt` (workspace roots + db paths)
- `roundtrip_evidence.json` (stable-field evidence)
- `restore_verify.json` (verification results)
- `backup_restore_e2e.log` (timestamped steps + durations)

## Logging contract
- Every step logs:
  - scenario name
  - workspace path
  - command invoked (redacted)
  - pass/fail with durations
- On failure, print artifact pointers and a short summary.

## Testing
- Determinism:
  - no fixed sleeps; waits are explicit and bounded
- Safety:
  - scan all artifacts/logs for secret-like strings (reuse the redaction scan approach from `wa-4vx.10.18`)
  - ensure backups do not embed secrets in clear (especially config)
- Correctness:
  - verify round-trip evidence (token searchable; stable counts)
  - verify corruption rejection and no partial restore

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Round-trip export/import preserves core data.
- [ ] Corrupt backups are rejected with clear errors.
- [ ] Pre-import safety backup is created and verifiable.
- [ ] Metadata-only export exists and is smaller.
- [ ] Logs/artifacts are detailed, redacted, and sufficient to debug failures.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:56:30.14421545Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.304547-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-29T03:08:49.571125579Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-v40a","depends_on_id":"wa-2vr1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:31Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-v40a","depends_on_id":"wa-mxzp","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:31Z","created_by":"import"}]}
